conversation.message: Honestly, I don't know why I'm even here. I've tried quitting before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I just don't see the point in trying again.

You've been struggling with quitting for 20 years, and it can be really tough to feel motivated when it hasn't worked out before. What comes to mind when you think about your past attempts to quit, and what might have contributed to them not being successful?

I don't know... I just couldn't do it. I tried nicotine gum, prescription medication, and even cold turkey. But I always ended up going back to smoking. And it's not just that... I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always been conscious of my weight, and the thought of gaining even a few pounds is unbearable to me.

You've tried a variety of methods to quit, but still find yourself returning to smoking. Can you tell me more about what happens when you're thinking about gaining weight - is it a specific situation or feeling that makes you want to smoke?
I'm not sure... I've tried to diet before, and it never works out. And besides, I don't think I'm ready to quit smoking right now. I'm just not in the right mindset.
I understand that you're feeling uncertain about quitting right now and that dieting hasn't worked out for you in the past. What do you think would make you feel ready to quit smoking?
I suppose... but I'm not making any promises. What kind of small steps are we talking about?
I'm glad you're open to exploring small steps together. What situations or times of day do you usually find yourself reaching for a cigarette?
Okay, fine. Let's get this over with. But like I said, I'm not making any promises.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit resistant to the idea of working on quitting right now. What's going on that's making you feel like you're just going through the motions and not really committed to making changes?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not really interested in quitting. I've tried before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just a part of who I am.

It sounds like quitting feels like a daunting task to you, especially after trying before without success. Can you think of a specific situation or event that made you feel like smoking was necessary, like when you were stressed or around others who smoke?

Well, I've tried nicotine gum, patches, and even cold turkey. But every time, I end up feeling anxious and depressed. It's like my mental health gets worse when I try to quit. And on top of that, I always gain weight when I quit, which is really frustrating.
You've mentioned that quitting makes you feel anxious and depressed, and also leads to weight gain. What are some specific situations or moments when you find yourself reaching for a cigarette?

Yeah, I've talked to my doctor. They just tell me to quit smoking, like it's that easy. They don't understand how hard it is. And my family and friends don't get it either. They're all smokers, so they just think I should keep smoking and not worry about it.

You feel frustrated with your doctor and loved ones not understanding the difficulty of quitting. Have you ever considered saying no to a cigarette when someone offers it to you, and if so, how did that go?
I don't know...I just don't think I can do it. I've failed so many times before.

You've been trying to quit smoking for a while now, and it can feel really discouraging when it doesn't work out. Since you've tried strategies like nicotine gum, patches, and cold turkey, what small change do you think you could make in your quitting approach that might help you feel more in control this time?
I...I guess so. But I'm not making any promises.
It sounds like you're willing to take a small step towards quitting, that's a good start. What would be a good day for you to start reducing your smoking, so we can work together to make a plan?

Honestly, I'm feeling really frustrated. I've been trying to quit for weeks now, but it's just not sticking. I've been smoking for about 10 years, and I know it's not good for me, but it's hard to stop.
I can sense your frustration, it sounds really tough to quit. How do you usually feel when you're struggling to resist the urge to smoke?

Well, I've been feeling really guilty about smoking around my niece and nephew. They're always asking me why I smell like smoke, and it breaks my heart. I want to be a good role model for them, and I don't want to die young from some smoking-related illness.
It sounds like being around your niece and nephew makes you feel guilty and motivated to quit. How do you usually feel when you're at home with them, and you're craving a cigarette?
Hmm...I'd say about a 6? I mean, I've tried before and failed, so I'm not sure if I can really do it this time.
Rachel, you've had a history of trying to quit in the past and not being successful. What do you think might be some of the specific situations or feelings that make you reach for a cigarette?

I think I need more support. My friends and family are all smokers, so it's hard to stay motivated when everyone around me is still smoking. And as a freelancer, I work from home, so I don't have coworkers to support me either.

Rachel, you mentioned needing more support, which is really important for staying motivated. Would having a daily check-in or phone call with someone, like a friend or family member, help you feel more accountable and motivated to stay smoke-free?
Yeah, I've thought about it, but I don't know...I'm not really sure where to start or if it would even help.

Rachel, considering you've mentioned work stress as a trigger, would it be helpful to imagine and prepare for how you'll manage those feelings when you're working from home, even if it's just mentally rehearsing some coping strategies?
Yeah, okay. I guess it couldn't hurt to try.
It sounds like you're feeling more hopeful about taking the next steps towards your goals.

Honestly, I'm not really sure why I'm here. I mean, I know I should quit, but it's hard. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit a couple of times before. It didn't work out. I guess I'm just worried about the cost of quitting. I've heard those prescription medications can be really expensive.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit uncertain and overwhelmed about quitting, and the cost is one of your main concerns. What are some of the specific situations that make you want to smoke right now?

Well, like I said, the cost is a big deal. I'm not sure if my insurance will cover the medications or the nicotine replacement therapy. And even if it does, I'm not sure if I can afford the copays. And then there's the fear of failure. I've tried to quit before, and I didn't make it. I don't want to disappoint my family and friends again. As for my smoking habits, I usually smoke about a pack a day.

It sounds like the cost and fear of failure are significant concerns for you. Can you tell me more about what happens on a typical day when you're smoking a pack a day - where and when do you usually smoke?
Okay, that makes sense. I didn't realize my nicotine dependence level was that high. And yeah, let's talk about those options. I want to know what might work best for me.

You're feeling more informed about your nicotine dependence, and now you're considering the different treatment options. Can you tell me more about what's going on at home - how do your family and friends feel about your smoking, and how might their support (or lack thereof) be affecting your decision to quit?
Okay, that sounds good. I think I'd like to try the nicotine replacement therapy. And what about counseling and support groups? How do those work?

I'm glad you're interested in trying the nicotine replacement therapy. What situations or emotions usually make you want to smoke, and how do you think you'll handle those triggers when you're trying to quit?
Yeah, I knew it was bad for me, but I didn't realize it was that bad.
You're acknowledging the risks, that's a great step. Can you tell me more about what usually triggers you to smoke, like when you're stressed, bored, or around certain people?
Yeah, that sounds like a good plan. I can do that. Thanks for listening and understanding. I feel like I can actually do this this time.
Now that you feel more confident about quitting, can you think of a specific situation or time of day when you usually smoke the most cigarettes?
Honestly, doc, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think I need to quit smoking. It's not a big deal. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I'm fine.

You're feeling like you don't need to quit, but that's not how your body seems to be responding, given your 15 years of smoking. What do you think is driving your desire to keep smoking, even if you're not feeling like you're in immediate danger from it?
Quitting? Nah, I don't think so. I've tried it before, and it didn't work out. I don't want to gain weight, and I don't want to give up the social aspect of smoking. It's part of who I am, you know?

You're worried about giving up the social aspect of smoking and gaining weight. What are some specific situations where you find yourself wanting to smoke with others, and how do those interactions make you feel?
I don't know...I've seen friends who quit and they just ballooned up. I don't want that to happen to me. And what about when I'm out with friends at the bar? Smoking is just part of the experience.

It sounds like those social situations, especially at the bar, are a big concern for you. What do you think would happen if you politely said no to a cigarette offer or decided not to go to the bar for a while, especially in the early stages of quitting?
It's just...I'm a bartender, and smoking is part of the culture. My friends all smoke, and it's just what we do. If I quit, I'd feel like I'm missing out or something.

That sounds like a big part of your social identity and routine, and it can be hard to imagine doing things differently. Would you be willing to try identifying specific moments when you feel like you're missing out because you're not smoking?
I guess so...but I'm still not sure I want to quit.
It sounds like you're still uncertain, but also open to exploring some alternatives. What was it about your previous attempts to quit that you think might be helpful to try again this time around?
Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit before, but I started again. I don't know why I can't just stay quit. It's like I'm stuck in this cycle.

It sounds like you're really struggling with feelings of frustration and being stuck in a cycle of quitting and starting again. Can you think of a specific moment or situation that led you to start smoking again this time?

Well, I was doing okay for a while. I had cut down to just a few cigarettes a day, and I was feeling pretty proud of myself. But then I started getting really stressed with work, and I just fell back into old habits. I'd take a break, and the next thing I knew, I was smoking again. It's like my brain just automatically goes to cigarettes when I'm feeling overwhelmed.

It sounds like you were making good progress before stress at work pulled you back in. What's going through your mind right now when you think about quitting for good - what's holding you back, and what's pushing you forward?
Hmm, I don't think I've tried those before. How do they work?

I'm glad you're considering new options, but let's take a step back and explore why you want to quit smoking in the first place. What's the most important reason for you to reduce your smoking - is it your health, your family, or something else?
Okay, that sounds like it could be helpful.
I'm glad you're open to exploring options. What's the most important reason for you to reduce your smoking - is it to improve your health, for your family's sake, or something else?
Yeah, that would be great.

You're open to considering tips on managing weight gain, which is a big concern for many people when quitting smoking. What do you think is the biggest challenge you face when it comes to changing your eating habits and exercise routine?
I suppose I could try to cut down to just one cigarette break a day instead of two or three. That might be a start.

You're considering cutting down to one cigarette break a day, which is a positive step. What's driving you to want to cut down, is it a specific health concern, a desire to save money, or something else?
Yeah, that sounds good. I think I need all the help I can get right now.

You sound really overwhelmed and willing to seek help, which is a huge step already. What's driving your desire to cut down on smoking, is it a personal goal, a family member's concern, or something else?

Honestly, I'm not doing great. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I've tried to quit a few times, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself. I feel like I should be able to quit, but it's just so hard.

It sounds like you're really struggling with quitting, and you feel like you've failed yourself. Can you tell me more about what happens when you go back to smoking - is it always after a specific situation or feeling, or does it just seem to happen randomly?

It's just...I have depression and anxiety, and smoking seems to help me cope with those feelings. But at the same time, I know it's making things worse. And I've tried to quit before, but I always gain weight, and that just makes me feel even more miserable. And my friends and family all smoke, so it's hard to resist the temptation. And to top it all off, my partner doesn't support me in my quit attempts, so I feel like I'm doing it all on my own.

Rachel, I can see how your emotions and relationships make it tough to quit. Have you considered writing down why quitting is important to you, and keeping that list somewhere visible to remind you of your motivation?

Well, when I'm feeling anxious or overwhelmed, smoking just seems to calm me down, you know? It's like a temporary escape from my emotions. But I know it's not a healthy way to deal with things, and it's just making everything worse in the long run.

It sounds like smoking is acting as a temporary relief for your anxiety and overwhelm. Have you ever tried using deep breathing exercises to calm down, or would you be open to trying something like that to manage your emotions?
Yeah, I guess I never thought about that. I've always just focused on quitting smoking, but maybe I need to focus on dealing with the underlying issues first. That makes sense.

It sounds like you're starting to understand that addressing your underlying issues might be key to quitting smoking. Would you be open to learning a technique that can help you politely decline cigarettes when offered by friends or family, so you can avoid situations that might trigger a craving?

Honestly, I'm not really sure. I mean, I know smoking's bad for me and all, but I've been doing it for years and I'm not really sure I'm ready to quit. Plus, I've got a lot of other stuff going on in my life right now.
Alex, it sounds like you're feeling a bit uncertain about quitting, and that's okay. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke in a day?
I guess I just feel like it's not good for me, and I should probably stop. But like I said, I've got a lot of other stuff going on, and I'm not sure I'm ready to take on quitting smoking too.
You feel like quitting is the right thing to do, but you're not sure if you're ready to take it on right now. How long have you been smoking?
Yeah, I've heard that before, but I'm not sure I believe it. I mean, I've smoked for years and I'm fine.

I see you're still unsure if quitting smoking will make a difference in your life. 

Have you noticed any times when you feel like you're smoking out of habit, rather than because you really want to?
Yeah, I guess so. I'm worried that quitting will make my depression and anxiety worse.
I can see why you'd worry about quitting affecting your medication. Have you ever noticed any changes in your mood or anxiety levels when you're under more stress, like when you're smoking?
Okay, that sounds like it might be helpful.

Let's discuss strategies to help you cope with cravings and stress while quitting smoking. Would you be open to trying a technique that involves talking to me when you feel a strong urge to smoke, to see if it helps reduce the intensity of the craving?
Yeah, I knew that. But like I said, I'm worried about gaining weight if I quit.

Now that we've discussed some of the potential benefits of quitting, let's talk about how you can cope with cravings when you're feeling stressed, which is a big trigger for you. Can you think of a time when you managed to deal with stress without smoking?
Okay, I think I'm willing to listen and learn more.

Given that you're in the Planning phase, I should focus on suggesting a relevant coping strategy or technique based on the patient's specific triggers and past attempts.

Since the patient is open to learning more and has expressed concern about stress triggers, I'll gently introduce a technique that has shown to be helpful for many individuals.

Therapist: You mentioned that stress is a big trigger for you, Alex. Have you ever tried talking to someone about your cravings when you feel stressed, or sharing them with a trusted friend or family member?

Honestly, I'm really struggling. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit three times before. The longest I lasted was three months, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself.
It sounds like quitting has been a really tough and frustrating experience for you. What usually happens right before you reach for a cigarette, or right after you've had one?

My daughter. She's getting older, and I don't want her to see me smoking. I'm worried about the example I'm setting for her. And to be honest, I'm also worried about my health. I've been experiencing some breathing issues lately, and I know it's related to smoking.

I can see that your daughter's influence and your health concerns are strong motivators for quitting. Who in your life could be a supportive person you could call or talk to when you're feeling like smoking, especially in the morning when you mentioned you have cravings?

Well, that's the thing. I don't really have one. My husband is a social smoker, and my friends all smoke. They don't understand why I can't just cut down instead of quitting altogether. And my work schedule is so busy, I don't have time for support groups or counseling.
Who in your life could be that person for you, someone who understands your struggle and can offer support when you need it?
Yeah, I've been using some online forums and apps, but it's hard to connect with others. It feels like I'm going through this alone. And sometimes the advice they give just doesn't resonate with me.

That feeling of being alone can be really tough. Given that you mentioned your previous quit attempts didn't last long, I want to try something different this time. Have you ever talked to someone about your cravings, like a friend or family member, to see if that helps you feel more connected and makes the urge to smoke a little more manageable?
Cravings. I get these intense cravings, especially during stressful work meetings or when I'm driving. And sometimes I just give in to them.

You've mentioned that cravings are a big challenge for you, especially during stressful work meetings and when driving. Have you ever tried changing your environment when you get one of these cravings, like stepping outside for some fresh air?
Yeah, I'm willing to try anything at this point. I just want to quit for good this time.
You've taken a huge step forward today – I'm confident in your ability to move forward with a healthier path.

Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two years, but then I started again after a night out with friends. Now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day. It's frustrating because I know I can quit, but it feels like I'm stuck in this cycle.
I can sense how frustrated and discouraged you must feel with this cycle of quitting and starting again. What's the one thing that keeps you from giving up and trying to quit for good this time?

Well, I was out with friends at a bar, and we were all having a few drinks. It's just so easy to smoke when I'm drinking. I feel like I can relax and be myself around my friends when we're all smoking together. It's like it's a part of who I am when I'm socializing.

I can sense the strong social connection you feel with your friends when you're all smoking together. How does quitting smoking make you feel about potentially distancing yourself from this social aspect of your life?

Honestly, I don't know. I feel like I'd be the only one not smoking, and it would be awkward. Plus, I'm worried that if I quit, I'll just get anxious and irritable, and that would be even worse than smoking.

That sounds like a pretty tough spot to be in. Would you be open to trying a simple approach where you practice politely saying no to social offers of cigarettes and remind yourself why you decided to quit, to help build your confidence in staying smoke-free?

I guess it would be better for my health, and I wouldn't have to worry about the smell or the cost. But it's hard to see past the short-term struggles. I just feel like I've failed already, so why bother trying again?

It sounds like you're worried that past attempts at quitting won't help you this time, but I'd like to flip that around - what small thing from those past attempts can you build on this time, so you're not starting from scratch?

Look, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think quitting is a big deal. I've been smoking for 25 years, and it's not like it's killing me or anything. I enjoy it, it helps me relax, and it's just part of who I am.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit uncertain about why you're here and whether quitting is worth it for you. What's going on in your life right now that might be making you even slightly consider quitting, no matter how small?

Well, like when I'm dealing with a difficult project or client, it calms me down. And when I'm on a break at work or waiting for a job to start, I just smoke out of habit, I guess. And, you know, when I'm with friends or at parties, it makes me feel more relaxed and social. But, honestly, I don't think I can quit. I've tried before, and it didn't work. I don't think I can do it.
You've been trying to quit before, and it didn't work. What do you think is the biggest challenge that made it difficult for you to quit the last time?

Oh, I don't know... I guess I'd have more money, and my wife would be off my back about it. But, to be honest, I don't think it would make a huge difference. I've got other issues, like my depression and anxiety, that are way more important to deal with. And, like I said, smoking helps me cope with those things. Plus, I don't think I can quit without quitting drinking and pot too, and I'm not willing to do that.

Alex, it sounds like you're using smoking as a way to cope with your depression and anxiety, and that's a big part of why you're drawn to it. Can you tell me more about what it would mean to you to find other ways to manage those feelings, rather than relying on smoking?
I don't know... I'm not sure I believe that. But, I guess it couldn't hurt to talk about it. But, don't expect me to quit anytime soon.
I hear that you're skeptical about quitting smoking, but willing to explore the idea. What's holding you back from committing to trying to quit, even if it's just for a short period of time?

Honestly, I'm feeling a bit overwhelmed. I've been trying to quit for a while now, but it's just so hard. I've tried cold turkey, nicotine gum, and even prescription medication, but nothing seems to stick. I'm worried that I'll never be able to quit.
It sounds like you've been feeling really stuck in your attempts to quit. What are some of the situations or emotions that usually make you reach for a bidi?

Well, I know it's bad for my health, and I'm worried about the impact it's having on my lungs and heart. But to be honest, it's also the stress and anxiety that comes with it. I use tobacco as a way to cope with my emotions, and I'm not sure what I'll do without it. I'm afraid that quitting will just make things worse.

You're really acknowledging how hard it is to quit, and how you've used tobacco to cope with stress and anxiety. Can you think of any other times in your life when you've managed your stress and anxiety without smoking, even if it was just for a short time?

Honestly, I'm struggling. I quit about a month ago, but it's been tough. I've been using nicotine gum, but I still find myself thinking about smoking all the time. And to be honest, I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always had a hard time with my weight, and I'm scared that quitting smoking will make it worse.
It sounds like you're really struggling with cravings and weight concerns are weighing on your mind. Can you tell me more about how you think about smoking when you're using nicotine gum?

I guess I just got tired of feeling like a slave to cigarettes. I was smoking 15-20 a day, and it was taking a toll on my health and my wallet. And to be honest, I was getting tired of the smell and the way it made me feel. But it's hard because I really enjoy smoking. I miss the social aspect of it, and I miss the ritual of it. It's hard to imagine relaxing without a cigarette.

You miss the social aspect of smoking and the ritual of it - that must be really hard for you. Can you tell me more about what specific situations or moments make you reach for a cigarette, even with the nicotine gum helping?

I think it would be amazing. I would feel so much healthier, and I would be proud of myself for overcoming this addiction. But I'm scared that quitting will make my depression and anxiety worse. I've struggled with those conditions for a while, and I'm worried that quitting smoking will make them harder to manage.

I can see that you're really scared about quitting smoking affecting your depression and anxiety. Have you considered talking to your doctor or a therapist about how quitting smoking might impact your mental health, and what strategies we can use to manage any potential challenges?
Yeah, I think that would be helpful. I just feel like I'm stuck and I don't know how to move forward.
I totally hear that feeling of being stuck. To move forward, let's start thinking about a specific situation where you usually feel like smoking, like when you're feeling anxious or depressed.
Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two weeks six months ago, but I ended up starting again. It's just so hard. I feel like I've failed.
I can imagine how tough it must feel to have tried quitting before and fallen back into old habits. What is it about quitting that's keeping you from giving up, even after feeling like you've failed?

Yeah... I went out with friends to a bar, and we were all having drinks and laughing... and someone offered me a cigarette. I didn't even think twice about it. It was like my brain just shut off, and I was back to my old habits. And then, of course, one cigarette turned into a pack, and now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day.

It sounds like you felt really caught up in the moment and lost control when your friends offered you a cigarette. What's been going through your mind since you went back to smoking - is there anything that's been holding you back from quitting again?

I know it's bad for me, and I hate the way it makes me feel... guilty, anxious, and out of control. But at the same time, I really enjoy smoking. It's hard to explain, but it's just something that makes me feel relaxed and happy. And my friends all smoke, so it's hard to resist the temptation when we're together.

You're struggling with the mixed feelings about smoking, feeling guilty and anxious, but also enjoying it for relaxation. What do you think is holding you back from quitting, if it's not just the enjoyment of smoking itself?
I guess so... I just don't know if I can do it. I feel like I've tried everything already.

I can sense your uncertainty and frustration about trying again. What is it about wanting to quit that's more important to you right now, is it the health benefits, your family's well-being, or something else?
Yeah, okay. I guess that sounds like a good place to start.

That's a great starting point, let's focus on making progress rather than perfection. What are your biggest concerns about quitting this time around that might make it harder for you to stay on track?
Look, I know I smoke a lot. It's just something I do. I don't really see the big deal. My friends and family all smoke, so it's just a normal part of my life.
You mention that smoking is just a normal part of your life. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke in a day?

It's just something I've always done. I started in college, and it's just become a habit. I don't really think about it that much. And like I said, all my friends do it, so it's not like I'm going to quit just because you're telling me to.
You've been smoking since college, and it's become a habit. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke per day?

You don't understand. If I quit, my friends will think I'm lame or something. They'll stop inviting me to hang out. And what am I supposed to do instead of smoking when we're out at the bar? Just stand there and do nothing?
How long have you been smoking, Alex?

I guess not, really. I mean, I've tried to quit a couple of times before, but it didn't last. And yeah, it would be nice not to have to worry about finding a place to smoke or having to leave the bar to go outside. But I just don't know if it's worth it.
Let's focus on gathering more factual information about your smoking habits. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke per day?
I guess so. But I'm not promising anything.
You're not promising anything, and that's okay. Can you tell me how many cigarettes you typically smoke in a day, Alex?

Honestly, it's been tough. I quit a few weeks ago, but I'm still struggling. The anxiety and irritability are killing me. I feel like I'm on edge all the time. And working at the pub doesn't help. Everyone's always smoking and drinking, and it's hard not to join in.

I can see that quitting has been really challenging for you, and you're feeling overwhelmed by anxiety and irritability. You've already made some progress by quitting a few weeks ago, which shows you're capable of change. Can you tell me what motivated you to quit in the first place?

The hardest part is dealing with my emotions. I feel like I'm a different person when I'm not smoking. I'm short-tempered, and I just want to lash out at people. And the cravings are constant. I'll be in the middle of a shift, and suddenly I'll smell smoke and it's like my brain is screaming at me to go grab a cigarette.

I can see that quitting is really affecting your emotional well-being. You mentioned feeling short-tempered and irritable, which must be really tough for you. What motivates you to keep going and try to stay smoke-free, even when it feels so challenging?

Yeah, I've tried some stuff. Deep breathing, meditation... but it's hard to do that when you're in the middle of a busy shift. And to be honest, I'm not sure I believe in that stuff. I just feel like I need a cigarette to calm down.
I can see that you're really struggling with trusting those relaxation techniques, and that's okay. What specifically is it about deep breathing and meditation that doesn't resonate with you?
Hmm, that's a good point. If I'm not drinking, I might not be as tempted to smoke. But it's hard for me to imagine not having a drink after work. It's just what I do.
You're really attached to having a drink after work, aren't you? What's the main reason you usually have a drink after your shift - is it to relax, unwind, or something else?
Yeah, I think I could do that. One shift a week, that's doable.
You're feeling hopeful about taking that first step. What is it about quitting smoking that's become more important to you now - is it your health, your family, or something else?
Yeah, that would be great. Thanks for listening and understanding. I feel like I'm not alone in this anymore.
Now that you feel more hopeful, what is it about quitting that's really important to you, and what's driving your desire to stop smoking?

Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for a few months, but I started again. It's just so hard. I enjoy smoking, you know? It's one of the few things that helps me relax and takes my mind off things.
I can see you're really struggling with this, and it's okay to feel that way. What makes you think quitting is worth trying again, given how hard it's been for you in the past?

Well, I like the taste, the smell... it's just a whole experience, you know? And it's social, too. I work in events, so I'm always around people who smoke. We'll take breaks together and chat while we're smoking. It's a way for me to connect with my colleagues and friends. Plus, it helps me focus and calm down when things get crazy.

You've shared with me all the reasons why you enjoy smoking, and it's clear that it's a big part of your social life and stress relief. What do you think would happen to your relationships with your colleagues and friends if you were to quit smoking?

I don't know... I guess I just need to find something else that gives me that same feeling of relaxation and social connection. But it's hard, because nothing else seems to do the trick. And I'm worried about gaining weight if I quit. I've tried before and I ended up putting on a few pounds. That's just another stress I don't need.

You've mentioned that you've tried quitting before and gained weight, which can be really stressful. Have you considered replacing your daily smoking breaks with short walks or light physical activity to help manage stress and possibly even prevent weight gain?

Actually, yeah... there are a few people I work with who don't smoke. Maybe I could try to connect with them more during breaks. That might help. But what about when I'm feeling stressed and overwhelmed? That's when I really need a cigarette.
Given your concerns about feeling stressed and overwhelmed, have you ever tried doing something physical to calm yourself down, like taking a short walk?
